planting.note: Direct sow in fall or start transplants after cold stratification in spring in very well-drained, dry to moderately dry, rocky or sandy, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; cylindric blazing star demands exceptionally sharp drainage and will rot in wet, clay soils.

directions: Direct sow in fall or start transplants after cold stratification in spring in very well-drained, dry to moderately dry, rocky or sandy, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; cylindric blazing star demands exceptionally sharp drainage and will rot in wet, clay soils. Liatris cylindracea is the smallest and most delicate-looking liatris, native to dry, open, rocky prairies and limestone glades of the Great Lakes region and northeastern prairies; it produces small, cylindrical (rather than spherical) flower heads in bright rose-purple from July through August on slender stems, blooming from the top down in the characteristic liatris fashion. An outstanding choice for dry rock gardens, gravel borders, and xeriscape gardens in the northern portion of its range; it is important for specialist native bees and migrating monarch butterflies.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall or plant transplants in spring in very well-drained, dry to moderately dry, sandy to rocky, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; scaly blazing star is more tolerant of clay soil than some liatris species but still requires adequate drainage to prevent crown rot.

directions: Direct sow in fall or plant transplants in spring in very well-drained, dry to moderately dry, sandy to rocky, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; scaly blazing star is more tolerant of clay soil than some liatris species but still requires adequate drainage to prevent crown rot. Liatris squarrosa is immediately distinguished from other liatris species by the conspicuously reflexed (squarrose), long-pointed bracts of its large, solitary or sparsely clustered flower heads, giving each flower a spiky, star-burst appearance; it produces bold, rosy-purple to rose-pink flowers from July through September on stiff, upright stems. Native to dry, sandy to rocky, open habitats from Delaware to Georgia and west to Kansas, it is an important early fall nectar source for migrating monarch butterflies and specialist native bees. More heat-tolerant than many liatris species and a good choice for zone 7-9 dry gardens.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall or plant transplants in spring in well-drained, dry to moderately moist, average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; eastern blazing star is adaptable to a range of dry to medium-dry garden conditions and is one of the easiest blazing stars to establish in the Eastern garden.

directions: Direct sow in fall or plant transplants in spring in well-drained, dry to moderately moist, average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; eastern blazing star is adaptable to a range of dry to medium-dry garden conditions and is one of the easiest blazing stars to establish in the Eastern garden. Liatris scariosa is one of the showiest of the eastern blazing stars, producing very large (up to 1.5-inch), rounded, button-like purple flower heads on tall stems from August through September; the large flower heads make it easy to identify in the field and give it exceptional ornamental impact compared to the more narrow-flowered species. Native to open woods, rocky slopes, sandy barrens, and open glades from New Hampshire to Georgia, it supports specialist native bees and is an important fall migration nectar source for monarch butterflies; it is the primary blazing star for ornamental use in eastern garden designs.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall or start transplants after cold stratification in spring in well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loam soil in full sun; meadow blazing star is adapted to the Great Plains and northern prairies and performs best in cool to moderate summer climates in zones 2-7.

directions: Direct sow in fall or start transplants after cold stratification in spring in well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loam soil in full sun; meadow blazing star is adapted to the Great Plains and northern prairies and performs best in cool to moderate summer climates in zones 2-7. Liatris ligustylis is recognized by the Xerces Society and monarch researchers as the single most important plant for migrating monarch butterflies in the northern Great Plains — individual plant blooms in late summer to early fall (August-September) coincide precisely with the peak of the southward monarch migration route, and blooming plants can attract hundreds of monarchs simultaneously. It produces large, rounded, rosy-purple flower heads on sturdy stems and is outstanding for ornamental prairie and meadow gardens in the upper Midwest and northern Plains; it is increasingly difficult to find in commerce but is one of the most ecologically valuable native prairie plants that can be cultivated.
